parts.answer: The forces act on the same object. (1) They are different types of force. (1) 2

Downward arrow labelled magnetic force (from disc) Or Downward arrow labelled F. (1) Upward arrow labelled (normal) reaction / contact / R / N / and arrow length approximately equal to F + W. (1) 2 Total for question 11 4 Number Answer Mark

It’s the force / stress beyond which the cable does not return to its original length when the force / stress is removed (1) Use of ε = Δx/x (1) ε = 0.021 (1) 2 Example of calculation ε = 0.126 m ÷ 6 m = 0.021 Use of σ = F/A (1) σ = 1.4 × 10 Pa (1) 2 Example of calculation σ = 1.34 × 10 N ÷ 9.6 × 10 −3 m = 1.40 × 10 Pa Total for question 12 5 Number Answer Mark

(1) (1) Horizontal arrow pointing to the left labelled "reaction (force from the wall on the hook)" Arrow drawn below screw and not lower than last point of contact between hook and wall MP2 depends upon MP1

Use of moment of force = F x (1) Use of principle of moments (1) Use of W = m g (1) Correct calculation leading to conclusion no (as maximum is two coats) (1) 4 Example of calculation 150 N × 0.009 m = W × 0.025 m W = 1.35 N m ÷ 0.025 m = 54.0 N m = 54.0 N ÷ 9.81 N kg −1 = 5.50 kg 5.50 kg ÷ 2.6 kg = 2.12 ⸫ two coats max, so no. Total for question 13 6 Number Answer Mark

Use E K = ½ m v Use of efficiency = useful energy output total energy input Efficiency = 0.56 Or 56% Example of calculation E K = 0.5 × 1 560 kg × (13 m s −1 ) = 1.32 × 10 J efficiency = 73.9 × 10 J ÷ 1.32 × 10 J = 0.56 (1) (1) (1) 3 As the velocity increases the drag forces increase. (1) At higher speeds more work done against air resistance. (1) So more energy dissipated. (1) So a smaller proportion of energy is available to charge battery. (1) (Hence) the efficiency of the system is lower. (1) 4 Total for question 14 8 Number Answer Mark 15* This question assesses a student’s ability to show a coherent and logically structured answer with linkages and fully-sustained reasoning. Marks are awarded for indicative content and for how the answer is structured and shows lines of reasoning. (1) • When upthrust equals/exceeds weight of bulb / disc, bulb floats (1) • Weight of a bulb / disc is constant (1) • As temperature increases the weight of displaced liquid decreases (1) Or As temperature increases upthrust on bulb decreases • Until weight of a bulb / disc exceeds the upthrust (1) • There is a resultant force and bulb will sink (1) Total for question 15 6 Answer shows a coherent and logical structure with linkages and fully sustained lines of reasoning demonstrated throughout. Two arrowed lines for 70 N and 24 N with correct orientation (1) [use template for angle] Lines labelled with name/force/scaled length (1) Tension drawn in correctly i.e. correct vector diagram with correct direction (1) Answer in range 55 ± 2 N (1) 4 [Correct answer from trigonometry scores MP4 only] Example of calculation

Measure angle of string to vertical (1) Using a protractor (1) Calculate weight of mass holder and masses using W = mg (1) Vertical component of T is equal to W (1) Vertical component is T cos θ, so T can be calculated (1) 5 Total for question 16 9 Number Answer Mark Use of p = m v (1) Use of conservation of momentum (1) m = 151 (kg) (1) 3 Use of F = Δp / Δt scores MP1 and MP2 Example of calculation p = 250 kg × 2.10 m s −1 = 250 kg × 1.15 m s −1 + m × 1.57 m s −1 = 525.0 kg m s −1 m = (525.0 − 287.5 N s) kg m s −1 ÷ 1.57 m s −1 = 237.5 kg m s −1 ÷ 1.57 m s −1 = 151.3 kg No external horizontal forces acted on either car during the collision. (1) Use of a = Δv / Δt (1) Use of ΣF = ma (1) ΣF = 1.76 × 10 N (ecf from (a)(i)) (1) 3 Example of calculation average acceleration = 1.57 m s −1 ÷ 1.35 s = 1.16 m s −2 ΣF = 151.3 kg × 1.16 m s −2 = 1.76 × 10 N

P exerts a force on Q so Q exerts a force on P (1) Due to N3 forces are equal and opposite in direction (1) Resultant force on P opposite to direction of motion so according to N2, P decelerates (1) 3 Total for question 17 10 Number Answer Mark
Use of trigonometry (1) Vertical component = 34 (m s −1 ) (1) 2 Example of calculation 52 m s −1 × sin 41° = 34.1 m s −1

Method 1: Use of s = u t + ½ a t with s = 11 m and a = − 9.81 m s −2 (1) Use of quadratic formula (1) t = 6.62 (s) [Allow ecf from (a)] (1) Method 2: Use of v = u + at, with v = 0 to find time to max height [3.48 s] (1) Use of s = ½(u + v) t, or other correct suvat equation, to find max height [59.3 m] And Use of s = u t + ½ a t to find time to fall to 11 m [3.14 s] (1) t = 6.62 (s) depending on rounding of (a) [Allow ecf from (a)] (1) 3 (allow ecf from (a)) [Allow any valid suvat method] Example of calculation Let time to max height = t 11 m = 34.1 m s −1 × t − ½ × 9.81 m s −2 × t 4.91 × t − 34.1 m s −1 × t + 11 m = 0 t = (34.1 ± √( 34.1 − 4 × 11 × 4.91) m s −1 ÷ 9.81 m s −2 = 6.62 s (or 0.34 s)

Resolves for horizontal component of velocity (1) Use of s = v t (1) 260 m so no (1) 3 (Allow ecf from (b) with correct conclusion based on student’s value) Example of calculation Horizontal component of velocity = 52 m s −1 × cos 41° = 39.2 m s −1 s = 39.2 m s −1 × 6.62 s = 260 m Distance required 245 m to 255 m and 260 m > 255 m so no. Total for question 18 8 Number Answer Mark

The viscosity decreases (with temperature) (1) Use of V = (4/3)π r (1) Use of ρ = m / V (1) Use of W = m g (1) W = 4.76 × 10 −3 N (1) 4 Example of calculation volume = (4/3)π × (3.5 × 10 −3 m) = 1.80 ×10 −7 m m = 1.80 ×10 −7 m × 2.70 × 10 kg m −3 = 4.85×10 −4 kg W = 4.85×10 −4 kg × 9.81 N kg −1 = 4.76 × 10 −3 N Use of F = 6π η r v (1) Use of U = weight of fluid displaced (1) Comparison of F with W − U and conclusion consistent with student's values (1) 3 Example of calculation D = 6π × 0.95 Pa s × 0.003 5 m × 0.040 5 m = 2.54 × 10 −3 N U = 1.80 ×10 −7 m × 1.26 × 10 kg m −3 × 9.81 N kg −1 = 2.22 × 10 −3 N W − U = 4.76 × 10 −3 N − 2.22 × 10 −3 N = 2.54 × 10 −3 N 2.54 × 10 −3 N = D ⸫ Stokes law obeyed Low speed Or Laminar flow Or Small sphere [Accept reference to wide cylinder] (1)

Viscosity of blood is much lower (1) Drag will be lower for given velocity (proportional to diameter) (1) Reducing diameter gives less weight (proportional to cube of diameter) (1) Forces balance at lower speed Or Terminal velocity lower (1) Laminar flow needs low speed (1) 5 Viscosity of blood much lower (1) (For the original sphere) drag would be (much) lower at same velocity (1) So terminal velocity would be (much) too large for Stokes' law (1) Reducing r reduces W much more than D OR W proportional to r3 but D proportional to r (1) (With smaller sphere) forces will still balance at low speed (1) Total for question 19 14
